Tafseer:
The eloquence of the oath by the dawn:
As noted in the introduction, the oaths sworn in this Surah serve as testimonies to prove the claim that the true Controller of this universe is Allah, the One without partners. In His hand lies the rein of everything. When He wills, He makes something appear, and when He wills, He makes it disappear. He grants respite to whom He wills, and restrains where He wills. Nothing can cross the boundary He has prescribed, or interfere with His authority.
The purpose of the dawn's testimony: 'By the dawn' (wa-l-fajr) - 'Fajr' refers to the time when the veil of the night's darkness is torn and the light of day appears from the eastern horizon. Regarding the rules of fasting, Surah Al-Baqarah states:
(and eat and drink until the white thread of dawn becomes distinct to you from the black thread [of night]) (Al-Baqarah, 2:187).
In contrast to 'fajr', the word 'subh' (morning) encompasses not only the dawn but also the time after sunrise. Therefore, where an oath is sworn by 'subh', explanatory words are added, such as:
(And by the morning when it breathes) (At-Takwir, 81:18),
or:
(And by the morning when it brightens) (Al-Muddaththir, 74:34).
Having established that 'fajr' refers to the beginning of the morning when the night's darkness is torn, the occasion of this oath is identical to what was stated in Surah Al-Muddaththir as:
(And by the night when it departs, and by the morning when it brightens—indeed, it [i.e., Hell] is one of the greatest [disasters]) (Al-Muddaththir, 74:33-35).
We explained under those verses that when the night's darkness is spread, there is no sign of the morning. The dawn brings a major change, wrapping up the darkness and presenting a new face of the world. The exact same is the case with the Resurrection. This world is like the night whose darkness covers the morning of the Resurrection. Just as the dawn appears after the night according to a specified schedule, similarly a time will come when the Resurrection will appear suddenly, and everyone will see what they regarded as impossible.
Through 'By the dawn', the Qur'an warns that the dawn daily displays a model of the Resurrection: just as you sleep during the night and rise in the morning rubbing your eyes, similarly you will rise on the morning of the Resurrection after having turned to dust, feeling as if you had just slept and were awakened. Therefore, do not regard the Resurrection as distant. The supplication taught in the Hadiths for rising in the morning also points to this reality.

The meaning of the ten nights:
'And [by] ten nights' (wa layalin 'ashr) - Which ten nights are meant here? Various answers have been narrated by the commentators, but none is supported by any reliable proof. They are based on the assumption that since an oath is sworn by them, they must be sacred nights, whereas this assumption is baseless. Oaths in the Qur'an are mostly sworn to serve as proofs of the claim. Instead of seeking sanctity, one should look at the argumentative aspect and see what the claim is and how the oath testifies to it, keeping in view similar passages of the Qur'an.
Keeping in view the core theme of this Surah, 'and ten nights' refers to the ten nights of the moon's waxing and waning. Since the word is in the indefinite form, it can encompass both the ten nights of waxing and the ten nights of waning, presenting a complete picture of the moon's gradual progress and decline. This is expressed in a simple style in Surah Ya-Sin:
(And the moon—We have determined for it phases, until it returns like the old dry palm branch) (Ya-Sin, 36:39).
In this verse, the moon is depicted as a compliant she-camel whose rein is in the hand of an unseen camel-driver, who guides it phase by phase to a peak of height, and then brings it down phase by phase until it becomes thin like a dry palm branch.
This same appeared in the form of an oath as:
(And [by] the moon when it becomes full, you will surely embark upon state after state) (Al-Inshiqaq, 84:16-18).
This warns those who are hastening the Resurrection: every sign of Allah has a specified program and gradual progress. The Resurrection is a requirement of His justice and is bound to happen, but will occur at its appointed time.
In the light of these precedents, the Qur'an has referred to the ten nights of the moon's waxing and waning because the change in the moon during these nights is highly prominent. This change is a proof that Allah Almighty has appointed a gradual progress for the manifestations and events of this universe. A pregnant woman requires a specified period to give birth. Allah does not seize the criminals immediately, but grants them respite, and He brings the punishment only when the respite ends. Similarly, this world is traveling toward the Resurrection and will reach it gradually, in accordance with the schedule He has appointed.

The meaning of 'even' and 'odd':
'And [by] the even and the odd' (wa-sh-shaf'i wa-l-watr) - 'Shaf'' means even, and 'watr' means odd. What is meant by even and odd? Commentators have narrated so many opinions that it is difficult to count them. This is because they focused on finding a sacred entity for these words, instead of looking at the context. If they had looked at the argumentative aspect, they would have referred to the passages where the Qur'an mentions that He has created everything in pairs, pointing to important truths—for example:
(And of all things We created two mates; perhaps you will remember) (Adh-Dhariyat, 51:49).
Every pair is composed of two odd parts—meaning everything is odd from one perspective, and even from another. Referring to this fact of nature, the Qur'an has drawn attention to various pairs in the universe—heaven and earth, darkness and light, heat and cold, male and female—pointing to important truths, as indicated by: 'perhaps you will remember'. One truth is that since everything possesses a void that is filled only by its pair, without which it cannot fulfill its purpose, there must exist a perfect and complete Being, free of any need, Who establishes harmony and union among these diverse parts, making them purposeful. This Being must be perfect and free of need; if He lacked anything, He would depend on another, and this would continue indefinitely. Another truth is that this world, as a whole, possesses a void that is filled only by the Hereafter; without it, this world is not a wise work, but a vain plaything.
The mention of 'even' and 'odd' appears after the mention of the ten nights, which we interpreted as the phases of the moon. This bears witness that the rein of everything is in the hand of Allah, the One without partners, because during some months these nights are even, and during some they are odd. No one can turn the even into odd, or the odd into even, no matter how much they desire. People make various efforts to see the crescent of Eid on the twenty-ninth, but the moon obeys only its Lord's command, without caring for their desire.

The testimony of the night:
Finally, He swore by the night, specifically when it departs: 'And [by] the night when it passes' (wa-l-layli idha yasr). In the light of Qur'anic precedents, this points to important truths.
One truth is that all the diverse elements of the universe are subservient to the Creator. When the night comes, its darkness seems so absolute that no sign of day's light seems possible, yet the white thread of dawn begins to appear from the eastern horizon and soon overwhelms the night. The night cannot block its path.
Second, the night departing and the day appearing serves as a model of the Resurrection for those who regard it as impossible.
Third, for those who assume that the Resurrection is so distant that they need not worry, it is a reminder that in the divine schedule, this world has approached its end just like the night at dawn, when only a minor transition remains for the morning to appear. Man measures this with his limited scales of minutes and seconds, unable to comprehend how this world, which has stood for ages, can face an upheaval. But in the divine schedule, the Resurrection is close. The Prophet expressed this, saying: 'I and the Resurrection are like these two fingers.'
Furthermore, 'when it passes' (idha yasr) contains a subtle testimony: do not regard any trial as a permanent calamity; just as you witness the morning appearing and the night departing, and see that He does not allow either to remain beyond what is necessary for His world's welfare, similarly the trials of ease and difficulty, grief and comfort, are intended for man's mental and moral training, and Allah grants them respite only as long as necessary for his training. Man should neither despair nor flee, but face them with patience and gratitude, hoping that His trial contains goodness.
Reflecting on verses 1-4, the oaths 'By the dawn' and 'By the night when it passes' are of two contrasting things, and the intermediate oaths 'By ten nights' and 'By the even and the odd' relate to two great signs within these contrasting things, serving as proofs of the claim. Since the primary status belongs to the first and final oaths, and the intermediate oaths are their توابع (consequences), we also recall the claims Allah has proved through these contrasts of night and day, light and darkness:
- First, He proved monotheism from these contrasts—showing that although this world appears to be a battlefield of opposites, which should have prevented its existence or destroyed it immediately, these opposites share a deep harmony, cooperating to produce useful results that preserve the world.
Now, Who establishes this harmony among these opposites? The only correct answer is: an All-Knowing, Wise, and All-Powerful Being Who is superior to these opposites, Who joins them, creates a wise proportion among them, and produces useful results. He must be One; for if there were other wills opposing Him, the universe would have been ruined: (Had there been within the heavens and earth gods besides Allah, they both would have been ruined) (Al-Anbiya, 21:22).
- Second, He argued for the Resurrection from this. Briefly, He has created everything in pairs in this world, and everything reaches its purpose only by joining its pair. Without this, it remains incomplete and meaningless. This same applies to this world as a whole: it contains a void that is filled only by the Hereafter. The constant struggle between good and evil, justice and injustice in it requires a Day of Justice when its Creator judges people with complete power, knowledge, and justice, rewarding the righteous and punishing those who caused corruption. If this were not to happen, it would mean the Creator regards good and evil as equal, and His creation of the world is a plaything. Thus, those who deny the Resurrection are asked:
(Then will We treat the Muslims like the criminals? What is the matter with you? How do you judge?) (Al-Qalam, 68:35-36).
Refer also to what we wrote under Surah Al-Muddaththir.
- Third, He clarified that just as the light and heat of day, and the cold and darkness of night are necessary for physical life, similarly the trials of ease and difficulty, comfort and distress are necessary for man's moral and spiritual training, so that he develops patience and gratitude and becomes worthy of the status of a 'satisfied soul'. This will be discussed under Surah Ad-Duha.
- Fourth, He clarified that He has not left these opposites unbridled, but their reins are in His hand. They cannot deviate from their boundaries. They bear witness that they are not free, but are subservient. When the night comes, it does not remain forever, but must depart at its appointed time, with strict discipline. Similarly, the sun must set at its appointed time, and cannot remain over our heads forever. This is an obvious witness that everything is under His control, and no one should grow arrogant due to His respite, assuming they are secure. Since the sun, moon, night, and day are under His control, what is the status of man! This is expressed in Surah Al-Qasas as:
(Say, 'Have you considered: if Allah should make for you the night continuous until the Day of Resurrection, what deity other than Allah could bring you light? Then will you not hear?' Say, 'Have you considered: if Allah should make for you the day continuous until the Day of Resurrection, what deity other than Allah could bring you a night?') (Al-Qasas, 28:71-72).
Since this aspect is prominent in the oaths of this Surah, keep it in mind.

The natural requirement of being rational:
'Hijr' means intellect. 'Hijr' and 'aql' share a similar meaning—both containing the meaning of restraining and binding. Since intellect is the inner rein of man to restrain him from what is unworthy of him, it is described as 'hijr'.
The interrogative style of this verse contains both a severe rebuke and the establishment of proof. The meaning is: since Allah has blessed man with intellect, it is fitting for him to learn a lesson from the signs that are pointing to the path and destination, instead of insisting on believing only when the destination appears before him. Believing at that moment will bring him nothing but regret, which will be discussed under verses 23-24.
The proof is established because while there is no lack of signs, none of them became a source of belief for them. Now, He has presented those signs that are most obvious and close, containing a great testimony for the rational. But these stubborn individuals will not benefit even from them.

The testimony of history:
Following the signs of nature, this draws attention to the history of past nations—showing that the Creator of this world is not unconcerned with the conduct of nations, but is constantly watching them. When they deviate, He grants them respite in His wisdom to allow them to reform or fill their cup of sins. Then, He seizes them, erasing their existence. This conduct of Allah with nations is a proof that He will bring a Day of Recompense for this entire world, when every individual will be questioned. Then, the grateful will be rewarded, and the ungrateful will be punished.
'Aad' was mentioned in various places, and here they are mentioned in connection with 'Iram'. 'Iram' is their ancestor from whom their military and architectural progress began. History shows they belonged to the Semitic branch descending from Iram bin Sam bin Nuh.

'Imad' refers to lofty columns, serving as a metaphor for their architectural progress, just as: (and basins like reservoirs and stationary kettles) (34:13) appeared in Surah Saba to describe the generosity of Prophet Solomon.
Aad made great progress in the art of stone-carving, carving lofty and beautiful palaces within the mountains. Their wealthy individuals desired to construct a monument on every high ground. Their prophet, Hud (peace be upon him), rebuked them for this wasteful and ostentatious desire.

'The like of which had not been created in [other] lands' (allati lam yukhlaq mithluha fi-l-bilad) - Along with their architectural progress, they were also highly prominent in their stature, strength, and power. No previous or contemporary nation could match them.

After Aad, reference is made briefly to Thamud. They were from the survivors of Aad and inherited their love for architecture and taming the earth; hence, they were called Aad the Second. Their dwelling was 'Wadi-l-Qura', where they carved homes within the mountains following the way of their ancestors, pointed to by: 'who carved the rocks' (jaboo-s-sakhra).

After the ancient nations, reference is made to Pharaoh and his hosts. 'Dhi-l-awtad' literally means the owner of pegs, but 'awtad' (pegs) is used metaphorically for Pharaoh's hosts. Armies generally live in tents, which are established by pegs; hence, this metaphor is recognized in Arabic. The abundance of Pharaoh's hosts is mentioned in the Torah and the Qur'an. In ancient times, maintaining a standing army was not common; rather, tribal youth volunteered on occasions. But Pharaoh, as mentioned in the Torah, established a standing army that patrolled the kingdom in their tents. He also bound his lords to keep a specific number of weapons, horses, and chariots ready. Because of this specific characteristic, he was described as 'owner of pegs'.

The transgression of nations: This points to the attitude they chose after receiving power and resources from Allah Almighty. It is stated: they chose the way of transgression—meaning they became untroubled and independent of God, assuming that what they possessed was the fruit of their own capacity, in which they were free to act, and no one could strip it from them or question them.

'And increased therein corruption' (fa-aktharu feeha-l-fasad) - This describes the result of their transgression: having fallen into this delusion, they deviated from the straight path. They handed their reins to their desires and Satan, which resulted in corruption spreading in every aspect of their personal and collective lives.

The divine law regarding nations:
'So your Lord poured upon them a scourge of punishment' (fa-sabba 'alayhim rabbuka sawta 'adhab) - This describes the end of their corruption: when corruption spread in every aspect of their lives, its consequence appeared in the form of a scourge of punishment from Allah. The forms of this punishment were detailed in the previous Surahs.
Allah's law is that when He blesses a nation with power and choice, He does not leave them unbridled; rather, He watches how they use this power. If they use it within the limits He has established, they enjoy His favors; and if they transgress His limits, they are granted respite to reform or establish the proof. If they do not benefit from this respite but increase in transgression and corruption, Allah Almighty destroys them, because their existence is no longer useful for themselves or others. This decision of Allah is absolute, and no nation can prevent it. History bears witness that He seized many nations in their couth of power and erased them completely.

Summary of the discussion:
This summarizes the oaths mentioned in the introduction and the historical accounts mentioned above. This verse serves as the subject of the oaths, supported by both the signs of nature and the events of history.
'Mirsad' means a place of lying in wait. The meaning is: the signs of nature and history bear witness that the Creator has not abandoned the universe after creating it, but is watching everything. Nothing can remain hidden from Him, and the reins of everything are in His hand. If a nation chooses transgression, He grants them respite, but its limit is fixed. If they transgress this limit, He seizes them, and no one can save them from Him.
This is a clear proof that this world is not a plaything, but is a purposeful creation of a Wise and All-Powerful Being. His relationship with His creation and His law regarding nations bear witness that He has appointed a Day of Recompense when He will gather everyone, rewarding the obedient with His pleasure and punishing those who caused corruption. If this were not to happen, His creation of the world would be a vain work.

A delusion of man:
This exposes the delusion because of which those who receive blessings choose transgression, and those who are deprived fall into despair—namely: one who receives abundance of sustenance assumes he is highly valued by Allah; and conversely, one who faces restriction of sustenance assumes he is despised by Him. As a result, the first grows proud and transgresses, while the second loses hope, choosing paths that take him far from God and into the hands of Satan. However, whether a person faces tancy or abundance of sustenance in this world, it is neither for his honor nor for his humiliation, but as a trial. Allah blesses with abundance to test his gratitude—to see whether he grows proud and despises others, or remains grateful, obedient, and a helper of His servants. Similarly, He restricts sustenance to test his patience—to see whether he remains content, stable, and firm in his character, or loses hope. On patience and gratitude depends the stability of man's entire religion; hence, both are tested, and those who develop these qualities will attain the 'satisfied soul' and the status of 'pleased and pleasing' with Allah.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 15 and 16 was combined in the original text.]

Refuting the delusion:
What was stated above in a general form is addressed directly to the leaders of Mecca and Ta'if, warning them: your assumption that abundance of wealth is a proof of your honor is completely false. It is not for honor, but for trial—to see whether you become arrogant or use it to help and honor orphans and the needy. It is your own foolishness that you interpret His favor completely opposite. You should have become grateful, helping orphans and needy, but in your pride of honor, you look down on orphans and withhold help from the needy.
The status of orphans in society: The word 'ikram' (honoring) is used for orphans, showing that what is demanded is not merely giving them some charity, but giving them a status of respect in society. They should not be driven away, but treated with respect, knowing that His wealth is of value only when used to honor orphans. Otherwise, it is a source of misery and disgrace.

'Tahaddun' means urging and inciting one another. That is: regarding the needy, what is demanded is not merely throwing some morsels to them, but establishing a system in society where everyone helps them and urges others to do so, instead of being miserly and discouraging others to cover up their own miserliness.
'Ta'am' (food) is used here in a wide sense, representing all their essential needs.

The conduct of powerful groups in Jahiliyyah:
'And you consume inheritance, devouring [it] altogether' (wa ta'kuloona-t-turatha aklal-lamma) - 'Lamm' means sifting and gathering, and 'ta'kulun' means devouring. That is: in your love for wealth, you have become so blind that the powerful among you try to sift and devour the entire inheritance left by the deceased, depriving the weak heirs, including orphans and daughters. Although there was a rule for inheritance in Jahiliyyah, just as today some powerful individuals violate the clear rules of Islam to deprive the weak, similarly in Jahiliyyah, the powerful had established a rule where all rights belonged to the strong, leaving nothing for the weak.

'And you love wealth with immense love' (wa tuhibboona-l-mala hubban-jamma) - This reveals the cause behind this ugly character: wealth is the only value for you, while all other high values—humanity, nobility, justice, and mercy—have been suppressed by it. The love of wealth has covered your hearts so completely that no other high value can find a way.

Warning to the worshippers of wealth:
This reminds the worshippers of wealth of the Day when they will realize and regret that they did not spend their wealth in preparation for this Day, but that realization will be of no benefit.
'No! When the earth has been leveled-pounded' (kalla idha dukkati-l-ardu dakkan-dakka) - 'Kalla' refutes their vain assumption that receiving wealth is a proof of their honor, whereas everything in this world is granted solely for trial. One Day, everything will be devastated, and man will face only his deeds.
'Dakka-l-ard' means leveling all its high and low places. The state of the earth on the Day of Resurrection is portrayed in Surah Al-Kahf as:
(Indeed, We have made that which is on the earth adornment for it that We may test them as to which of them is best in deed. And indeed, We will make that which is upon it [into] a barren plain.) (Al-Kahf, 18:7-8).
The meaning is: everything on the earth is temporary adornment for trial, and one Day, all of this adornment will be stripped away. Neither its rivers and mountains, nor its palaces and gardens will remain, but only a level plain.

'And your Lord has come and the angels, rank upon rank' (wa ja'a rabbuka wa-l-malaku saffan-saffa) - That is: while today He is testing people from behind the veil, on that Day, the veil will be removed and He will appear with His angels. At that moment, the actual reality will be completely visible, leaving no room for doubt.
As for the question of how He will appear, this belongs to the conditions of the Hereafter which are from the allegorical verses; general belief in them is sufficient, and prying into them can lead to fitnah.

'And brought [within view], that Day, is Hell—that Day, man will remember, but how [i.e., what good] to him will be the remembrance?' (wa jee'a yawma'idhim-bi-jahannam, yawma'idhiy-yatadhakkaru-l-insanu wa anna lahu-dh-dhikra) - That is: along with Him, Paradise and Hell will also be made visible. Since the Surah is addressed to the opponents, He mentioned Hell first. Seeing it, those who lived heedless of the Hereafter will beat their heads, wishing they had prepared. It is stated: this was to be done in the world; since they did not prepare here, what benefit will their regret bring there!

[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 23 and 24 was combined in the original text.]
'He will say, "Oh, I wish I had sent ahead [some good] for my life!"' (yaqoolu ya laytanee qaddamtu li-hayatee) - This explains the remembrance mentioned in the preceding verse: on that Day, those who are mocking the warners today will say with deep regret: 'O, would that we had sent before some good deeds during our life in the world for this life of the Hereafter!' The meaning is: they will regret, but their regret will be of no benefit, as the time for action has passed.

This describes the severity of that Day's punishment: on that Day, His punishment will be such that no one can punish in like manner, and His binding will be such that no one can bind in like manner—stating the actual reality. This is because all worldly pleasures and pains are temporary and can be ended by death, but there will be no death to relieve them from the punishment of the Hereafter.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 25 and 26 was combined in the original text.]

Direct glad tiding to the people of Paradise:
This points to the glad tiding the people of Paradise will receive directly from their Lord. What deserves special attention is the address: 'O reassured soul' (ya ayyatuha-n-nafsu-l-mutma'innah), which points to the quality of the soul of those worthy of Paradise. Above, in verses 15-16, He described those small-hearted individuals who grew proud when blessed with ease, and lost hope when faced with tancy. Now, He describes those whose steps remained firm on the path of truth in both good and difficult circumstances. When blessed with ease, they regarded it as a trial of their gratitude, and instead of transgression, tried to pass the test by spending on His servants. When faced with tancy, instead of losing hope, they regarded it as a trial of their patience, striving to emerge successful so that they would not be disgraced before their Lord. Since their hearts remained content and stable in both ease and difficulty, their soul is described as 'the reassured soul', and they are the ones who will inherit Paradise.

'Return to your Lord, well-pleased and pleasing [to Him]' (irji'ee ila rabbiki radiyatam-mardiyyah) - This is the praise from their Lord. Addressing them, it is stated: 'Well done! The game you played in the field of trial has succeeded. Now, return to your Lord in such a manner that you proved you remained content with Him in all circumstances, and you became approved and pleasing in His sight. You had no complaint against Him, and He found no shortcoming in you. You are pleased with Him, and He is pleased with you.'

'And enter among My [righteous] servants, and enter My Paradise' (fa-dkhulee fi 'ibadee, wa-dkhulee jannatee) - That is: since you succeeded in the trial, enter among My chosen servants and enter My Paradise.
[This entry corresponds to the explanation provided in the previous verse, as the Tafseer for verses 29 and 30 was combined in the original text.]
